message.content:
What a great question!

Yes, vowels are actually vowels. In fact, the English alphabet is based on the letter "a" and its combination of letters to form words. For example, the letter "a" can be represented by:

1. The letter "b" (short version)
2. The letter "c" (long version)
3. The letter "d" (short version)
4. The letter "e" (long version)
5. The letter "f" (short version)
6. The letter "g" (long version)
7. The letter "h" (short version)
8. The letter "i" (long version)
9. The letter "j" (short version)
10. The letter "k" (long version)
11. The letter "l" (short version)
12. The letter "m" (long version)
13. The letter "n" (short version)
14. The letter "o" (long version)
15. The letter "p" (short version)
16. The letter "q" (long version)
17. The letter "r" (short version)
18. The letter "s" (long version)
19. The letter "t" (short version)
20. The letter "v" (long version)
21. The letter "w" (short version)
22. The letter "x" (long version)
23. The letter "y" (short version)
24. The letter "z" (long version)

So, the English alphabet is based on the letter "a".
